data.title: Solid Power: I View The Stock As A Call Option With A $2.50 Strike
data.description:
Introduction

Although I usually cover large caps, in the past year, my research has brought me to deal with a few energy-tech names with a speculative profile due to their early-stage phase. This is why, from time to time, you have seen me share my view on stocks such as SES AI, Electrovaya, Oklo, and SMR. It's a cluster of stocks that I have been researching and that requires a different valuation method compared to what we usually do. This time, we are going to deal with Solid Power (SLDP), another pre-commercial solid electrolyte business that presents a clear binary commercialization outcome (success/unsuccess).

Solid Power At A Glance

Solid Power is based in Colorado, where it operates two facilities. Its strategy in the battery industry is not to become a cell manufacturer, but to sell sulfide-based solid electrolyte material to other battery manufacturers and to OEMs. Solid electrolyte material can replace the liquid electrolyte, which is flammable. The advantage that is achieved is higher energy density and a longer battery life, with the important addition that these batteries are also safer. Currently, the company has only two pilot electrolyte lines, but it is building a continuous-manufacturing pilot line that should be capable of 75 metric tons per year by the end of 2026. As we read in the Q4 2025 transcript:

In 2025, we finished ordering long lead equipment and conducted detailed design for the line. We expect to install and commission this line by the end of 2026. This line is designed to support small volume customer programs and allow us to learn and optimize the manufacturing processes ahead of full commercialization.

Moreover, while the company is trying to develop and move toward the commercialization stage, its management has admitted that work still needs to be done before a product ready for the market is manufactured:

During 2026, we will continue to enhance our understanding of how electrolyte performs relative to other sulfide electrolyte products to enable customer success in utilizing our electrolyte. We believe this will support our efforts to develop competitive differentiated products and secure long-term customers. To support these efforts, we will utilize our Electrolyte Innovation Center, or EIC, to develop, improve and test electrolyte manufacturing processes as well as electrolyte products. We also intend to continue focusing on our cell research and development activities on improving our understanding of how and why our electrolyte performs in the solid-state cell and using that knowledge to help our electrolyte customers improve their cell development.

Among its most important partners, we find BMW, Ford, Samsung, and SK On, but the only one that gives near-term revenue visibility is the one with SK On, because there is a commitment to purchase at least 8 metric tons of electrolyte through 2030 for a minimum of $8.3 million.

On the other hand, Ford basically stopped its joint cell development project with Solid Power, which is not positive news.

Solid Power's Financials

We said the company is in the pre-commercial stage. At the end of FY25, it reported revenues of $18 million, grant income of $3.8 million, which led to total revenues of $21.7 million. Interestingly, its direct costs are $20.6 million, which means that the company currently has no margin. Its total operating expenses reached $122.6 million, which generated a $100.8 million operating loss.

These numbers are not that meaningful at this stage. But they show how long a way to go the company has before it creates a profitable structure at the gross profit level and before it can generate real sales.

Now, the real financials that matter when we deal with similar companies are found in the balance sheet. In short, we have to understand how long the company can survive until it becomes profitable.

At the end of FY25, Solid Power reported $21.6 million in cash and $229 million in marketable securities. Long-term investments stand at $87 million. This means that the total liquidity is around $337 million. However, in January 2026, the company announced a $130 million registered direct offering of 17 million shares.

The net proceeds were $122 million, which means that we should expect available liquidity to be around $459 million. If we consider that Solid Power guided for operating expenses of $100 million, we could guess that its total liquidity could be around $435-$440 million at the end of Q1 2026. For the purpose of our analysis, this means that Solid Power has about five years of runway, which is comfortable.

How should we think about this cash position? Well, basically it is a call option on solid electrolyte commercialization. With a market cap just shy of $720 million, we have an EV of $261 million for the technology that Solid Power is developing and for its portfolio of partnerships. This leads us to the valuation because Solid Power is the combination of a balance sheet and a claim on the fact that solid electrolytes will be commercialized in a few years.

Solid Power's Valuation

Since most of Solid Power's future will depend on output volume, I modeled three scenarios that are anchored on three different electrolyte volume outcomes. This could give us an idea of how much Solid Power could be worth.

Bear Base Bull
electrolyte volume (2030) 20-40 100-200 300-500
gross margin 15% 35% 55%
further required dilution $300M $125M $25M
implied equity value $150M $1,000M $4,000M
PV per share (15% discount rate) $0.30 $1.90 $8

If we assign a 35% probability to the bear case, a 45% to the base case, and a 20% chance to the bull case, we have a weighted PV of $2.56. Currently, the stock trades at $3.43, so I understand why Seeking Alpha issued a warning.

Let's go back to the idea of viewing the cash position as a call option. As a matter of fact, the whole stock is a call option because we have a floor, which is represented by the cash/share ratio, and an upside with no ceiling if a resolution event materializes, such as the announcement of a binding agreement. The time to expire is roughly 3 to 4 years, which is aligned with the timeline of the SK On partnership. Volatility is incredibly high, and this impacts an option's time value.

What about its intrinsic value? Well, it should be around $459 million, which is the "pro-forma liquidity" that we calculated above. This is equal to $1.93 per share (I am considering that right now the outstanding shares should be around 238 million and not 220 million as at the end of FY25). Since the stock is trading at $3.43, we have $1.5 of time value.

This means that roughly $330 million of the company's equity is the price that the market is giving to the time value of this "option." In this framing, we should understand that volatility is not exactly a bug, but it is actually a "feature" of this stock. Moreover, this gives us a calculable math that depends on the probabilities we assign to each scenario. So, when we price the stock, we are actually deciding what probability weights we are assigning to each scenario. Finally, the entry point is not found as a discount compared to the target price but as a function of strike coverage.

Basically, if we find the stock below $2.50, it means that the EV is close to zero and that the option is, in a certain sense, almost "free" because the balance sheet covers the whole purchase price. It's not a valuation argument, as you understand, but it is just option theory. Call options are good buys when time value is not worth much. But options are also killed if the underlying goes to zero. In this case, it would mean that the commercial franchise is worthless.

As a result, I don't think the stock is a "Buy" at $3.43, as I believe that, given the volatility, a more interesting entry point could be around $2.50. To me, with this setup, paying close to 0 for time value seems a necessary condition. But it is not a sufficient one because we still have to believe that the commercial stage will be reached. Therefore, I am currently "Neutral," but at $2.50, I believe Solid Power could be a defensible speculative bet for investors who are aware that the outcome, as we said in the introduction, is clearly binary.

data.title: State Street Q1 Results: Macro Tailwinds Justify The Rally
data.description:
Shares of State Street (STT) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining about 80%, recovering strongly from the April 2025 lows. The company’s low-risk business model, improved efficiency, and benefits from rising markets have all helped to support results and increase investor enthusiasm. Solid Q1 results added to that optimism Friday with shares up over 2% in early trading. I last covered State Street in January, rating them a “hold” but recommending buying at $127 or lower, which was reached several weeks ago. With the stock up to $145, that proved to be a good entry level, making now an appropriate time to revisit STT.

In the company’s first quarter, State Street earned $2.84, which beat estimates by $0.20 as revenue grew 16% to $3.8 billion. Alongside Bank of New York Mellon (BK), and Northern Trust (NTRS), State Street is one of the three main custody banks. These banks are unique institutions essential to the smooth operation of the US financial system, by holding assets on behalf of clients. Unlike traditional banks, they do not have retail banking or lending businesses, making them much less exposed to credit quality.

Speaking to its size, STT now has $54.5 trillion of assets under custody/administration. This was up 17% from last year, mainly thanks to strength in markets. It also has won about $2.2 trillion of assets over the past year, or a ~4% organic growth rate, a very healthy rate given the maturity of the business. As a result, servicing fees were up 11% from last year to $1.4 billion.

In other words, revenue grew ~5% below the pace of asset growth. While some of this reflects the timing of market moves intra-quarter (as clients are typically billed in arrears), fees do generally grow more slowly than assets, given the fierce competition for AUC. Plus, the business has significant operating leverage (when the market rises 10%, the cost of servicing those assets does not really change), so as client account values grow, STT’s effective fee rate tends to fall.

STT’s effective servicing fee rate is only about 1bp on assets, which again speaks to the operational and scaled nature of its business. Because of this, the company has been actively focusing on growing additional services to support earnings. In particular, FX trading has been a source of meaningful growth, up 24% to $435 million. The elevated volatility environment has been supportive, though I expect a deceleration in Q2 given a difficult comparison.

Elsewhere, its securities finance revenue was only up 2% from last year at $116 million, which was a bit disappointing relative to the performance at BNY Mellon. I would look to see this improve later this year. Its software unit generated solid growth of 7%, led by strength in SAAS offerings. Its backlog is up 11% from last year, which should support continued growth.

Separately, State Street has an investment management unit with a significant presence in ETFs. AUM is up about 20% from last year to $5.6 trillion, again thanks to rising markets. Revenue was up 23% to $724 million. Inflows were a strong $49 billion, and the popularity of ETFs have allowed STT to be a net winner within the investment management sector. Active flows have been generally challenging but were a positive $3 billion in Q1. In general, I expect outflows from active strategies and this to be a temporary reprieve. Over time, revenue should rise broadly in-line with the market.

Given the low-fee nature of its businesses, cost efficiency is critical, and STT has made meaningful progress here. Expenses were up 9% from last year, about 2% slower than fee growth. This has come even as it has spent 18% more on tech to upgrade systems, and I expect we will pass peak spending growth here in 2026. Headcount is down 2% from last year, which should drive savings later this year.

As a result, pre-tax margins were up 4% to 29% in Q1. I also closely track pre-tax income before interest to see the profitability of its operating model. It made $134 million in Q1 vs $108 million last year, or ~25% better. While there is still room to improve margins here, STT is safely profitable before interest income, an improvement from several years ago. This improves the resilience of its business and diversity of cash flows.

State Street also earns interest on cash it custodies on behalf of clients. Net interest income was especially strong, coming in at $835 million, up from $714 million a year ago. The 4% sequential rise in Q1 was surprisingly strong as Q1 has 2 fewer days, which typically cuts NII. Total deposits were up $15 billion from last year to $258 billion, and noninterest-bearing balances were at $29 billion, up 16% from last year and over 10% from Q1.

While I believed these balances have bottomed, the magnitude of the recovery in Q1 was surprising and contributed to the 6bp sequential rise in margins (“NIM”) to 1.16%. These balances can move quarter to quarter, and I expect to see some outflow of NIBs from this elevated level. As such, NIM will likely move back towards 1.10% going forward.

Unlike traditional banks, STT does not have a large loan portfolio. Of its $293 billion in assets, $100 billion is in cash, and over $100 billion sits in high-quality bonds (mainly government and agency debt), and it has just $49 billion of loans. Most of these loans are participations in loans to large investment grade corporations. It has limited private credit exposure, mainly $1.6 billion of BDC loans, or just 0.5% of its portfolio. These loans have 80% subordination, making them very loss remote. Indeed, during the quarter, it set aside just $16 million for credit losses, speaking to the low-risk nature of its balance sheet. I am very comfortable with STT’s asset portfolio.

STT continues to have a solid balance sheet. Its tier 1 common equity (CET1) capital ratio is at 10.6%, consistent with its 10-11% target. This enables ongoing capital returns, including $400 million of buybacks in Q1. I expect buybacks to reduce its share count by 3.5-4% this year. Shares also offer a 2.4% dividend yield, which I view as highly secure.

Back in January, I was targeting $11.50 in EPS (+/- $0.20) and $10.50 in capital returns. With the S&P over 7,000, fee revenue should continue to rise, and later Fed cuts (if any) should support NIM. Thanks to these macro tailwinds, I now expect at least $12.15 in EPS this year. At 12x earnings, I view shares as at fair value, given its still heavy-reliance on interest income and long-term fee rate pressure, partially offset by its low-risk balance sheet. As such, I remain a “hold” and view STT as a suitable position in long-term portfolios. I would want shares to pull back below $135 before adding more.

data.title: Camtek: Why I'm Calling A Buy At Current Levels
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The business of Camtek (CAMT) is actually quality control, they manufacture the systems that semiconductor companies use to identify defects and gauge precision at different phases of the chip-making process.

On the face of it, that may not sound very exciting. However, to me, the smaller, faster, and more complicated the chip, the higher the price of making a mistake.

That is what makes the tools of Camtek less of a commodity and more of a necessity especially in the field of advanced packaging where stacking and interconnecting dies need a degree of precision that cannot be eyeballed.

The early 2026 order announcements were what made me change my interest to conviction, with reservations. A tier-1 IDM has ordered Hawk systems to the tune of $45 million to use in AI applications, and first-quarter OSAT orders have reached over $90 million, the majority of which is associated with CoWoS-like packaging to use in similar applications.

The two pieces of data combined give me an idea that demand is not concentrated but broad-based. One huge customer acquisition is simple to ignore, two separate demand streams that target the identical set of applications are more difficult to brush off.

Assuming that dynamic is true (which I believe it will, at least in the near future with the current AI infrastructure buildout), then Camtek can be said to have the earning power to warrant a premium multiple.

Business Overview

The tools Camtek makes are used by the semiconductor companies to check and measure the chips as they are made. Chipmakers employ their machines to detect defects and ensure that chips are produced with utmost precision. Camtek is more or less a gateway to these manufacturers.

It seems that the trend of making things smaller without compromising reliability and performance that semiconductor companies are headed is becoming more difficult to get right, as the chips become smaller, faster and more sophisticated, it seems harder to make things right, which is why companies such as Camtek offer the inspection and metrology systems to ensure that quality does not decline, yields increase and avoid unnecessary costs that can affect the bottom line.

It is difficult to identify defects and errors with standard chip manufacturing; this is why in advanced packaging, which is taking a larger role within the semiconductor industry with performance requirements increasing, precision, quality and reliability take on even greater significance.

You may be a believer in digital transformation or not, but the fact remains that Camtek is directly involved in digital transformation as it assists semiconductor firms in producing more quality chips at a better rate. The reason is that in that way it would be more challenging and expensive to produce the next generation of digital products in large quantities.

Camtek has a wide product range, it has an inspection, 3D metrology and metrology portfolio, which covers sub-micron frontside defects, backside and edge inspection, micro-bump coplanarity and height and advanced packaging measurements, including RDL, overlay, TSV, topography and warp/bow.

What is important is that Camtek is not offering a tool to make a single step in a narrow step; it is increasingly becoming a part of the wider process-control burden posed by chiplets, HBM, and heterogeneous integration, where complexity is increasing, and defect tolerance is decreasing.

Eagle G5 introduced increased throughput and increased sensitivity and Hawk targets leading-edge advanced packaging with the capability to inspect and measure to 500 million bumps per wafer at small pitch. Camtek tools are becoming more applicable as the density of packaging, inspection pressure, and metrology pressure all rise. I believe this will provide Camtek with a valid roadmap to not only share gains but also better ASPs [Advanced Semiconductor Packaging], particularly when Hawk expands the company addressable market as the management plans.

The 2025 revenues are $496.1 million, non-GAAP gross margin is 51.6%, and non-GAAP operating income has grown to $149 million, which means that the growth is flowing through with operating leverage real, not taken by the cost base.

Camtek seems to be playing in one of the most intact pockets of semiconductor spending and maintaining the quality in its margins, which generally indicates real product differentiation, not merely some transient demand tailwinds. The danger, naturally, is that expectations have become closely tied to AI-assisted advanced packaging demand, so the company has fewer leeway in case of execution errors than a slower-growing tool seller.

Camtek's Financials

In the last three months of 2025, the company made $128.1 million, which was 9% more than the same time last year. The gross margin under GAAP was 50.0%, the gross margin under non-GAAP was 51.1%, the operating income according to GAAP was $33.1 million and net income according to GAAP increased to $35.9 million. The company also had $61.1 million operating cash flow in the quarter.

The company is expanding while maintaining a gross margin of approximately 50%, indicating that its products are not priced at zero and that its prices and mix are performing well. This is really important for semiconductor processes in areas where accuracy matters, and customers are willing to pay for performance.

One more thing I would like to say is that the quality of earnings is better than it appears on the GAAP headline. It seems initially that it is not good that full-year GAAP net income decreased to $50.7 million in 2024, compared to 2024, when it was $118.5 million.

However, this decline was largely due to a loss of capital in repurchasing a significant portion of the 2021 convertible notes of the company when it issued new 0% convertible notes that mature in 2030 in 2025.

That is, the decline in the GAAP earnings did not imply that the business was becoming weaker. When you compare the non-GAAP results, however, net income increased from $137.6 million to $159.0 million and diluted EPS increased from $2.83 to $3.26. I think that is the more obvious perspective of the business.

Such difference matters since the primary argument of this paper is founded on the quality of doing things, rather than accounting noise. The growth of Camtek was accompanied by improved margins and high cash conversion, which makes the growth story appear much more stable.

I am not too much concerned about the balance sheet either. At the end of 2025, Camtek had $851.1 million in cash, cash equivalents, short- and long-term deposits, and marketable securities. For a company this size, that's a lot of money to have on hand. This balance sheet is still very flexible.

Camtek is making clear that 2026 is a year of forward-growth and not a year of digestion. COO Ramy Langer said on the Q4 2025 earnings call that first-quarter revenue would be approximately $120 million, that in recent months confidence in the power of the second half has been bolstered, and that Camtek anticipated that 2026 would be another year of double-digit growth.

He also indicated that this confidence is based on the pipeline of order and backlog of the company and continuous communication with our customers, which is essential since it connects the perspective with the real demand indicators, including management optimism alone.

Such commentary is more contextual as it complies with the commercial data points that the company has already revealed. Langer also indicated that customers are testing Camtek in its capacity to “ship and install a double-digit number of systems within a relatively short time frame”, and CFO Moshe Eisenberg re-emphasized that the growth was to gain traction in the first quarter and a bigger growth in the second half of 2026.

Camtek Valuation

I believe that the most appropriate primary valuation technique of Camtek is a forward non-GAAP P/E, pegged to FY2027 earnings power, rather than trailing GAAP earnings and not a direct DCF.

Camtek is already a very profitable specialty semiconductor equipment company, so earnings-based valuation is suitable.

Meanwhile, the loss associated with the convertible note refinancing skewed 2025 GAAP net income, rendering the reported GAAP P/E a poor indicator of the business's true earning power. A DCF alone would also introduce excess sensitivity to terminal assumptions to a company that continues to enjoy the benefits of a robust but cyclical semiconductor upcycle. I think the best way is to appreciate Camtek based on the earnings potential it can produce when the current AI and sophisticated packaging demand is converted into a fuller run-rate year.

The behavior of the business also supports that decision. In 2025, Camtek generated record revenue of $496.1 million, non-GAAP operating income of $149 million, non-GAAP net income of $159.0 million, and $142.6 million of operating cash flow. These figures demonstrate that it is not a long-term growth story since the company has already implemented, scaled and profitably.

I would further say that a forward P/E structure is more suitable to the peer set. Big, high-quality semi-cap names such as Onto Innovation (ONTO) and KLA (KLAC) now trade at about 35x trailing earnings, and higher-growth metrology names can fetch more, provided that, and only if, the company continues to perform as per the advanced-packaging opportunity.

Camtek appears to be costly in comparison to its peers on a trailing GAAP P/E basis and it is. The current trailing GAAP P/E of CAMT is approximately 162.76x compared to approximately 86.51x of Onto Innovation (ONTO) and approximately 48.66x of KLA (KLAC).

That difference is nearly fully attributable to a single loss in 2025 in the refinancing of Camtek convertible notes, which caused the collapse of the GAAP net income and the trailing multiple to an eye-catching, yet misleading, level. And this is exactly why using trailing GAAP P/E to value Camtek at this point in time tells you very little about the true earnings power of the business.

ONTO has a forward P/E (non-GAAP) of approximately 36x and KLAC is trading in the same range (45.55x FWD P/E) which is in line with their position as larger, more diversified semi-cap platforms with lower growth premiums embedded in it.

In its turn, Camtek is smaller, more demand oriented in the advanced packaging and AI-oriented inspection, and is entering into a solid multi-year order cycle, all of which deserve a forward multiple premium over the peer average.

The first thing that should occur is that revenue must continue to grow in the double-digit rate at least over the next two years. This places this company in the range of $575 to $585 million in 2026 revenue with $675 to $690 million in 2027.

Not crazy numbers, when you consider how many orders are flowing in, how well the company leadership anticipates growth in the second half of the year, and the fact that they are in a very fast-growing segment of the semiconductor equipment industry (essentially, the machines that are used to assemble computer chip).

The company must also maintain margins to where they are currently, as that is where the valuation actually becomes justified. In the case of a Buy, I believe that in the next two years they should seek 51-52% gross margin and approximately 31-32% non-GAAP operating margin, which is crucial since the thesis is not merely that Camtek will sell more tools but rather that the company will be selling into applications where precision is a concern, where failures can be costly and where customers will pay on performance. In case that continues to happen, then incremental revenue will keep decreasing at desirable rates.

Finally, the company should transform such growth to approximately $4.80 to $5.00 of non-GAAP EPS by 2027. I believe that is the number that is really needed to have a Buy rating. Provided that Camtek can reach there, it could be valued as apply a 44x forward non-GAAP P/E, which would give it a value between about $211 and $220 per share, with a median price of $215.60.

At a share price of $176.51, the company has 22% left on the table. I will consider that a Buy, but not a high-conviction bet. A high-end multiple is something at which I feel comfortable, since Camtek has a smaller size than most of its counterparts, is more closely connected to advanced packaging and AI-centric complexity, and is starting 2026 in a strong financial position after 2025 ended with cash, deposits, and marketable securities of nearly $851.1 million.

That is, this Buy case is founded on Camtek demonstrating that the existing wave of demand would enable pushing revenue into the high $600 million-range and maintaining the margin structure that already makes the business special.

Risks to Consider

Cyclicality is the most evident threat in this case. Semiconductor equipment is not a stable, recession-resistant industry and Camtek is not exempted by that fact. AI infrastructure spending may slow either due to increasing interest rates, decreasing hyperscaler capex or a wider pullback in technology investment; and in any of these cases, order flow may slow more rapidly than the existing backlog would imply.

The second half-hike of the management is already already factored in my valuation such that any significant delay in that second half-hike will blow my 2026 revenue estimate at stake even before the year is half-way.

Customer concentration is another aspect that I would not blind my eyes. Camtek has a sizeable amount of recent order activity linked to few tier-1 IDMs and OSATs, which are predominantly used in AI-based advanced packaging programs. It is not necessarily a bad thing when the demand is running hot, but it implies that a change of priorities of at least one or two big customers will cause a disproportional effect on the revenue curve of the company in the short term.

Lastly, valuation is also a risk. Camtek is priced to execute further at a forward non-GAAP P/E of 44x at a price of $176.51. Not much disappointment is added to that multiple. Should there be a light coming in of earnings, should there be a squeeze in margins, or should the market re-rate high-multiple semi-cap stocks down, then the stock might lose a significant part of its recent gains before the business behind it has done anything wrong.

Key Takeaways

At the end of the day, Camtek (CAMT) is one of the more interesting names I have looked at in the semiconductor equipment space in some time.

The business is already executing at a high level. The order flow is real, the margins are clean, and the balance sheet (with $851.1 million in cash and equivalents at year-end 2025) gives management flexibility that most smaller semi-cap companies simply do not have.

That said, I want to be clear about what this rating is and what it is not. A Buy on Camtek at current levels is a thesis built on execution and continued (actual) profitability.

The company needs to compound revenue at a double-digit rate through at least 2027, hold margins in the 51% to 52% gross range, and deliver somewhere in the neighborhood of $4.80 to $5.00 in non-GAAP EPS.

If those things happen (and I think it can happen with the tailwind of AI spending reaching $2.53 trillion by 2027), I think shares are worth $211 to $220. If they do not, let's say margins slip or the AI packaging buildout slows, or if macro conditions tighten in ways that push customers to delay tool purchases, then the premium multiple I am using gets difficult to defend quickly.

The way I see it, the risk/reward is favorable but not lopsided. I am comfortable with a Buy.

I am not comfortable pretending there is no scenario where this thesis gets challenged. For investors who can accept that kind of nuance, I think Camtek is worth owning at current levels. For those who need a wide margin of safety before committing, waiting for a better entry point or waiting for it to trade lower is a perfectly reasonable alternative.

data.title: Microsoft: Not Like Meta In 2022
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) stock has had a grueling last six months, with the stock losing over 35% from its peak in October to the low at the end of March.

While the stock is finally staging a rally, particularly over the last few trading sessions, now up 17%+ from its low in late March, I don’t believe this will be durable.

In many ways, Microsoft stock’s decline since late 2025 resembles that of Meta Platforms (META) in 2022, when its stock crashed over 60%+ through the whole year, as investors rejected the idea of the company investing its capex dollars towards building a "metaverse."

While Meta successfully turned its fate around by pivoting its narrative to “generative AI” in 2023, along with CEO Mark Zuckerberg announcing “Year of Efficiency," which brought life back into its business and allowed the stock to stage a V-shaped rally, I don’t see that playing out for Microsoft at the moment.

At a fundamental level, its Azure business is declining at a time when its competitors are seeing sequential acceleration in their cloud businesses. Not to mention, Microsoft's structural lag in its custom silicon roadmap is starting to show in the differences in results between Google GCP (GOOG) and Amazon AWS (AMZN).

At the same time, Anthropic's (ANTHRO) surge in revenue to $30B+ ARR is highly indicative of market share losses for its M365 suite, and I don’t see an inflection there either.

To validate my concerns further, current forward revenue estimates by consensus show no signs of revenue acceleration in the coming years as well, which is concerning.

As a result, I have been trimming my Microsoft position into the current bounce and will downgrade the stock to a Hold at current levels.

Azure Declines While Competition Accelerates

When Microsoft reported its Q2 FY26 earnings, its Azure business declined on a sequential basis to 38% (down from 39% in the previous quarter) and is expected to slow even further to 37.5% in Q3.

While management conveyed that the slowdown in the pace of Azure growth is driven by capacity constraints and a strategic decision to rebalance its compute capacity towards its higher-margin products like M365 Copilot and GitHub Copilot, investors were not convinced.

Especially when its competitors Amazon AWS and Google GCP grew their revenues at an accelerated pace quarter over quarter by 24% and 48%, respectively, as can be seen below.

Furthermore, what also drew a lot of investor scrutiny was the concentration of Microsoft’s backlog towards OpenAI (OPENAI), which currently stands at 45%, raising potential execution risks down the line.

Looking forward, Azure is expected to further slow down in the company’s upcoming Q3 earnings, where management projects a YoY growth rate of 37.5%. In the meantime, Amazon is projected to further accelerate its AWS revenues by 28-29% as I wrote about in this post, with no signs of slowdown over the next few quarters, while Google Cloud Platform is projected to grow at 44-50%.

What is also possibly hurting Microsoft in a structural way is that the company is considerably behind Google and Amazon when it comes to its custom silicon progress. While Google is undeniably the leader in its vertically integrated full-stack approach with its TPUs, we also have evidence that Amazon too is pushing in the same direction, as was revealed in their recent Shareholder Letter. As I explained in my earlier post, Amazon management claimed that the annual run rate for their chips business (inclusive of Graviton, Trainium, and Nitro) is now over $20B, with the possibility that they could be selling racks to third parties in the future.

However, the important thing to note is that aside from the multi billion dollars in revenue that these custom silicon businesses are bringing to Google and Amazon, they also accompany significant cost advantages, thus allowing these companies to save several billion dollars in incremental capex and capture operating leverage.

When it comes to Microsoft, we don’t have evidence yet of how their custom silicon Maia is translating into revenue and cost advantages for the company yet, leading investors to shift their capital away from the company and towards the other two hyperscalers instead.

Anthropic Eating Microsoft’s Lunch

Turning our attention to Microsoft’s Productivity & Business Processes segment, which contains its M365 suite, the growth rate has been stagnant and is projected to remain that way in Q3, with revenue growth estimates for M365 Commercial Cloud growth at 13-14%.

In the previous quarter, management laid out that Microsoft had reached 15M paying subscribers for Copilot, marking a 4% conversion of their 400M Office base, while also announcing plans to raise prices across their M365 suite for enterprises and consumers starting July 1, 2026, that would offer Copilot bundles with the base license.

Note that on May 1, Microsoft will launch its E7 Frontier Suite priced at $99/month that is built specifically for organizations that want to deploy AI at scale without managing multiple add-on licenses. Note that Frontier Suite would include Copilot license as well as provide model optionality to users between OpenAI’s GPT-5 and Anthropic's 3.5/4 models.

However, on this front, Microsoft faces a dilemma, like I discussed in my previous post, whereby on one hand it wins on infrastructure by hosting the models, but loses on the interface.

That is particularly true as Anthropic is growing its revenues at an unprecedented rate, reaching $30B in ARR recently and overtaking OpenAI in the process, indicating potential market share losses for Microsoft as enterprise users pivot to Claude for their enterprise AI needs instead of upgrading to premium M365 plans.

Per this LinkedIn post, Anthropic’s Claude Cowork is also being perceived as a more intuitive "agentic" tool that executes complex, multi-step tasks autonomously, in contrast to M365 Copilot, which is more of a “suggestion engine” that requires manual intervention.

YR Research on Seeking Alpha also pointed out how Anthropic's $30B ARR surge spells trouble for the M365 suite, and I strongly agree with his views as well.

The way I see it, Microsoft seems to have lost the plot, and an inflection in revenue for its M365 Suite seems unlikely at the moment.

No Signs Of Revenue Inflection In Sight, Despite Massive Capex

This brings me to the conversation around capex.

For FY26, Microsoft’s capex is projected to reach $120-146B, up from $88B in FY25, with two-thirds of it being allocated to GPUs and CPUs to meet short-term AI demand, while the remaining third goes towards building out data center sites.

Meanwhile, analysts expect Microsoft to reach its peak capex year in FY27 with projections in the range of $150-200B. However, given the timing lag of the revenue realization from the capex, margins are expected to compress from the heavy depreciation of the AI hardware.

Plus, should capex projections for FY27 surpass the higher end of the above estimate, it could lead to a period of potentially negative FCF, which would not be viewed positively by investors, in my opinion, especially as revenue is not projected to catch up till then.

Comparing this to Meta in 2022, the stock had suffered a historic decline, as investors did not buy into the company’s massive capex plans to build the Metaverse, especially as its user growth had stalled, along with macroeconomic and competitive pressures that had depressed investor sentiment.

However, after suffering a historic decline in stock price, Meta successfully did two things at a fundamental level that helped the stock stage a V-shaped recovery in 2023.

First, CEO Mark Zuckerberg declared 2023 as the “Year of Efficiency," implementing massive cost-cutting measures that included layoffs. Microsoft could embark on a similar mission at this point, given the efficiency gains of AI across the enterprise, which could help protect its margins to a certain extent.

Second, Meta had successfully pivoted its narrative from “building the metaverse” to "GenAI," whereby it unlocked a gold mine by developing AI-powered tools like Advantage+, that ushered in a new era of revenue reacceleration and margin gains for its FoA (Family of Apps) segment.

Unfortunately, when it comes to Microsoft, its forward revenue growth rate projections do not indicate a meaningful reacceleration, which is somewhat concerning, especially given the magnitude of capex invested, as you can see below.

The stock may be trading at a 20% discount to its 5-year P/E average at 25x, while Wall Street price targets estimate a potential upside of 37% to the average price target of $579 per share. But I don’t see an environment where the stock sees a fundamental rerating in its P/E ratio until it can demonstrate meaningful signs of revenue reacceleration.

In this case, I believe Microsoft stock will likely run into resistance at the 200 DMA at $472 per share before heading lower, which also coincides with market weakness that I’m anticipating into the summer.

My final verdict and conclusions

I am using the current bounce in the index as well as in the stock to slowly start trimming my position and will continue to do so until I bring my allocation size down to an “underweight” position of no more than 3-4%. This is the case, as I believe that Microsoft seems to have lost the plot in the short term, with both its Cloud business and Enterprise suite showing weakness.

In the meantime, I will also downgrade my rating on the stock to a Hold, as I anticipate further weakness in the summer that could create newer lower lows in the stock price for 2026.

data.title: Tesla: More Good News, And Markets Noticed
data.description:
Thesis Summary

In my last article on Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), I outlined why I continued to be bearish on the stock, even though I was looking at some positive developments in the company’s outlook.

Over the last 30 days, we have continued to see some improved news for Tesla, and this time the market seems to have noticed.

The stock is up 11% in the last week, and we may finally be seeing a shift in the technicals.

The rollout of the Tesla Semi, the AI5 Chip and full self-drive ("FSD") in Europe are all positive catalysts that could have a meaningful impact over the coming months.

I remain bearish, but could begin to turn bullish if we break above key resistance and if the next quarter presents more positive news for the business.

The Decoupling: Why Bad Deliveries Don’t Matter

Tesla recently released its Q1 2026 delivery figures.

The company delivered 358,023 vehicles, down 14% QoQ, and also short of analyst estimates of 380,000.

While you’d expect this kind of news to hit the stock, Tesla’s shares pretty much bottomed after the release, and have since rallied strongly.

This is good news for investors, since the shares seem to be decoupling from the auto business, which is struggling.

Tesla has its sights set on much loftier goals, and so do investors.

The Semi: From Pilot To Production

In my March piece, I highlighted the impressive efficiency results from the Tesla Semi pilot with Mone Transport.

As always, Tesla moves fast, and now the Semi, which opens up a $2 trillion market, has begun production.

Tesla has officially broken ground and fast-tracked the dedicated Semi factory in Nevada.

The factory will be located alongside the already existing Giga Nevada battery facility, allowing Tesla to benefit from vertically integrating the most important part of the truck, the battery cell.

Tesla plans to scale to 50,000 units annually by 2027, and this could be a very meaningful source of income.

If the math works out, which it does based on current data, then the Tesla semi could scale very quickly within the trucking industry.

The AI5 "Tape-Out"

The AI5 is Tesla’s big gamble.

As I also highlighted before, the AI5 will underpin Tesla’s main growth initiatives over the coming decade, including FSD and the Optimus, so proving the company can design a best-in-class chip is a must.

Tesla’s AI5 chip has now reached the "tape-out" stage, which means the chip has been fully designed, at least on paper.

Now it is ready to move into production. The hard part is arguably done, and now we wait for the final results.

In theory, the AI5 is rumored to be 10x more efficient than its predecessor. If this is true, then Tesla will have gone a long way in decoupling itself from Nvidia (NVDA) and should help the company achieve its lofty AI goals without having to spend as much money.

Not that $20B in CAPEX isn’t already a lot.

FSD Crosses the Atlantic

For years, FSD has been limited to North America, but that changed last week.

The Netherlands' RDW (Vehicle Authority) recently granted a "Type Approval" for Tesla’s FSD Supervised.

The Netherlands itself is already a sizable market, but more importantly, it opens up the door to this regulation being accepted across the whole EU and unlocks a massive monetization lever.

Tesla also has millions of cars on European roads to which it can’t sell FSD.

If even a small portion of these, say, 10% subscribes to FSD v14 at a monthly rate of €100, the impact on Tesla’s bottom line would be significant.

That would equate to around 200,000 new FSD subscriptions and just over $260 million in ARR.

Q1 Earnings: What To Expect

The whole narrative around Tesla will once again be put into question.

Beyond what we have already discussed, investors will be keen to hear about the wind-down of the Model S and X, which could help sustain the high CAPEX Tesla is looking to deploy.

We will also get to hear more about the Optimus Gen 3, and should in fact get to see a first prototype.

Ultimately, CEO Elon Musk needs to thread the needle. Keep investors excited about future projects, while also showing them that the core business is healthy enough to sustain this without too much cash burn.

What To Do About Tesla

Tesla's growth opportunities can't be ignored, and although the company is still expensive by any traditional valuation metric, investors are starting to get tempted.

Tesla is down over 30% from its all-time high, and that could already be seen as an attractive discount in relative terms.

From a technical perspective, this is the first time in months we have seen Tesla convincingly break above the 20 and 50 EMAs in the 4h chart, and that has shifted momentum.

I maintain my Sell rating, for now.

I can’t ignore the huge cash burn, the constant overspending and the deterioration of the brand image and core business.

But I am tempted to buy Tesla, and this could be good, perhaps for a trade. There are a lot of catalysts at play and a market that is returning to a risk-on dynamic, at least for now.

Final Thoughts

All in all, Tesla continues to be one of the most interesting companies out there, but perhaps also one of the hardest to value.

The market has priced in a lot of bad in the last six months, and now it's starting to price in some of the good, and the stock is reacting well.

I might consider dipping in for a trade if we get a clean retest of the key EMAs, but for now, my skepticism on Elon Musk and Tesla remains.

data.title: Corsair Gaming: The Hardware Upgrade Cycle Is Unavoidable Despite Near-Term Pressure
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Corsair Gaming (CRSR), I highlighted how the “AI Memory Demand And Weak Consumer Environment Create Long-Term Opportunity,” with Corsair still reporting double-digit growth and a solid foundation despite near-term pressure.

Following a strong quarter and year, Corsair remains a Buy despite their surge on earnings day, with the business showing fundamental improvements, standing to benefit from the inevitable hardware replacement cycle while still being in a solid position to navigate (pun intended) a very tough consumer environment, with rising pressure following the Iran conflict’s escalation despite tariff tailwinds.

Fundamentals Keep Improving

CRSR reported a very strong Q4 report, beating the market’s expectations and jumping ~25% on the day of the release, delivering a 12% increase in net revenue and 84% growth in Adj. EBITDA, with the FCF reaching $34.75 million in 2025, above the $26 million seen in 2024 but below the $76.39 million from 2023, as a result of a mix between macro pressure and a significant hit from working capital changes, while the management highlighted during their Q4 Earnings Call:

In line with the broader market, we did see softer holiday demand in North America in gaming peripherals, offset by stronger international performance. We expect demand to improve as we move through 2026, especially with the highly anticipated GTA 6 launch in Q4.

For 2026, Corsair expects between $1.33 billion to $1.47 billion in net revenue (vs. $1.47 billion in 2025), Adj. EBITDA of $100 million to $115 million (vs. $100.6 million in 2025), aiming at improving the quality of their growth through a focus on higher-margin categories, scaling their ecosystem platforms, supply chain management and growth in DTC, expecting another $12 million hit from tariffs in 2026, although that was before the Supreme Court's decision, so that's a potential tailwind.

Financially, based on Corsair’s latest report, we continue to see an excellent position, with a solid amount of cash available ($97.18 million), significant inventories, and the current assets alone covering their total liabilities, reducing their debt by over $52 million in 2025, further improving their position.

This is very important in the current environment, as we’re talking about a company with improved financials in the face of pressure from many fronts that can last for a couple more years, providing flexibility to allocate capital as they please.

This strong financial position can also make them a potential M&A target, with a larger peer that wants to gain exposure to the gaming/consumer segment potentially being able to deliver synergies through integrating the business and supply chains, standing to benefit from hardware replacement.

In fact, that idea was reinforced during their Q4 report, when Corsair also introduced its first-ever buyback authorization, with a $50 million program announced (7.33% total yield), adding another layer of flexibility for their capital deployment, with buybacks listed alongside organic growth, M&A, deleveraging and other capital returns to shareholders (e.g. potential dividends).

Corsair Gaming should release their next earnings report on May 6, with the market expecting Normalized EPS of $0.19 and $353.01 million in revenue, as their second half of the year and especially Q4 are generally the stronger ones, while early quarters are more about building the inventory and preparing.

Given the Supreme Court’s tariff decision and the ongoing Iran conflict, I will pay close attention to potential changes in guidance, with a tariff tailwind already identified above but also rising uncertainty due to the Iran situation, which can add pressure on the consumer at an already bad time.

Macro Pressure Ramps Up

Ultimately, the ongoing Iran conflict can add significant pressure to the company’s already shaky consumer environment, with the IMF and others already warning of a potential global recession if the situation doesn’t de-escalate quickly, while every extra day with commodities trading at high war premiums weakening it more and more.

It’s important to keep in mind that this is something that has implications well beyond “just” fuel prices, as they play a vital role in virtually everything else, potentially leading to significant inflationary pressure that can last for much longer than the conflict itself alongside higher shipping rates (costs), potential supply chain delays, and an inflationary wave that’s likely to pressure consumers even more at an already bad time.

Especially since Corsair depends a lot more on consumers than their peers (which are also strong in the business segment), the cyclicality of this business is even more evident, as the consumer environment will ultimately dictate their performance.

In today’s already weak consumer environment marked by significant trade-downs and potential delays in renewing older hardware, having a new wave of inflationary pressure can be terrible, especially if we see the Fed and other central banks decide to hike rates or at the very least pause them, which can further tighten budgets while prices are already high.

This can be just the cherry on top of the industry’s major near-term problems, being affected by tariffs and especially the global semiconductor shortages that affected several other peers. This comes as a result of companies diverting production efforts towards AI-related products with higher margins and higher demand, which further emphasizes the cyclical nature of this industry despite Corsair’s strategic investments in memory inventory to support the ongoing demand.

However, I’ll also note that the cycle of tech/hardware replacement can only be delayed so much before consumers would have to replace or upgrade their setups, which should be visible over the following years given the pandemic boost, especially given the advancements in tech seen since then. Sure, don’t expect this to be fixed overnight, with the company’s CEO even highlighting the potential to see this last for the next couple of years, which is in line with broader market expectations:

For us, the visibility in terms of the bigger pictures, we're getting this very similar information to everyone else in terms of fab capacity and AI data center consumption and current projection, I think everyone probably already see the same data is that the market will continue to be tight for the next couple of years. But for us, we don't just rely on output of semiconductors alone. We basically have a lot of ways to acquire inventories and produce inventory as we do have a manufacturing center in Taiwan that built DRAM modules. And the visibility that we use is basically just market intel out a couple of quarters, and we continue to operate based on the information that we use for the past 30 years, and that seems to be working so far.

Risks Reiterated

Due to their dependency on the consumer, Corsair will always depend significantly on the consumer and the broader environment, which is likely going to see significant pressure from the Iran-driven inflationary shock affecting it through much more than higher pump prices - and for much longer than the market may initially price in.

Meanwhile, the ongoing supply chain issues seen in the industry are expected to last for quite a bit, and even though they have their manufacturing center in Taiwan, this environment can still pressure margins and volumes alike, which is among the worst cases for a cyclical business like this.

Valuation

For CRSR’s DCF to Equity, assuming some working capital normalization after 2025’s big inventory build-up, the tariff tailwind, last year’s debt repayments reducing interest expenses, and given their guidance for higher EBITDA, I’ll assume a starting FCF of $75 million in 2026, without assuming any changes in net borrowings since their balance sheet doesn’t need it.

Given the industry’s pressure and potential to see delays in the consumers’ replacement cycle, I’ll assume this stays flat in 2027 and 2028, after which it would grow at a 4% CAGR until 2035 as the environment can recover, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a 12.5% discount rate to take into account the significant macro pressure and demand a higher margin of safety, as well as 107.8 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $762.76 million, for an intrinsic value of $7.08 per share, which is still above the current $6.39 at the moment of writing this despite assuming a strong margin of safety.

Conclusions

Corsair remains a Buy, backed by a strong business with great fundamentals that should be able to navigate an uncertain consumer environment and capture the inevitable hardware replacement cycle.

There are certainly risks to pay attention to, but the current valuation already seems to account for a significant margin of safety, still trading at an attractive price despite their recent surge.

data.title: Downgrading XMHQ To A Buy
data.description:
Introduction

In 2024, I initiated a Strong Buy rating for the Invesco Midcap Quality Index Fund (XMHQ). I believed then, as I do now, that XMHQ is a quality fund with a valid underlying methodology to guide one’s investment decisions. As the tools I use have grown, I can reassess my views on ETFs, mutual funds, and indexes to make a more robust decision about them. Today, I view XMHQ as a Buy, which is a downgrade from my original thesis. Notably, XMHQ has returned 6.16%, while the SPDR S&P 500 Trust (SPY) has returned 21.53%. As a disclosure, I own XMHQ, and I will continue to hold it for the foreseeable future.

The reasons to buy XMHQ will be discussed in detail below, but I want to remind any new readers that this recent bull market is long in the tooth. I have been predicting a deterioration in the returns that should continue through 2028, and have realized an anemic market by the end of 2026. Based on the Rule of 20, this market is completely overvalued, as indicated by a current P/E ratio of 30.24x and an inflation rate of 3.3%. Since that is an R20 of 33.54, that far exceeds the acceptable rate of 20.

Now, I never suggest an all-or-nothing approach to investing, and I do believe in proper asset allocation; it is past time to check allocations. After checking them, investors need to rotate investments out of large caps and consider mid-caps. The S&P MidCap 400 has a P/E ratio of 21.2x, which is 30% cheaper than the S&P 500. The constituents for XMHQ have a similar P/E ratio of 20.8x. When this market begins to crack, there will be a flight toward value, and mid-caps will be the place to go to find equity growth.

The Index

XMHQ uses the S&P MidCap 400 Quality Index as its underlying methodology. This decision was made in 2017, when it previously used the S&P MidCap 400 Equal Weighted Index. Even earlier, it had used two other indices since its inception. The current index uses three key factors to identify quality companies: return on equity, accruals, and financial leverage.
